Current measures charge flow rate; involves drift velocity and potential differences.
Electric potential energy equals work needed to assemble charges from infinity.
Charged object movement between different potentials changes potential energy.
Ideal conductors allow electrons to move freely throughout the material.
Parallel-plate capacitors have two oppositely charged parallel conducting plates.
Touching conductors redistribute charge until surfaces reach equal potential.
Charge is fundamental, scalar, quantized in units of elementary charge e.
Energy conservation determines maximum current in LC circuits.
Resistors dissipate energy stored in inductors during current changes.
Inductance opposes current changes; solenoid inductance depends on turns, length, area, permeability.
Induced currents in loops experience magnetic forces from external fields.
Faraday's law relates changing magnetic flux to induced emf.
Magnetic flux through constant field equals B·A with area vector perpendicular outward.
Biot-Savart law defines magnetic field magnitude and direction from current.
System charge changes via contact, friction, and electrostatic induction, including neutral systems.
Moving charges create magnetic fields dependent on velocity, distance, and perpendicular orientation.
Electric fields originate from charged objects.
Magnetic fields are vectors produced by dipoles, never monopoles.
Multiple capacitors combine into single equivalent capacitance using series and parallel rules.
Circuit energy changes occur as charges move through potential differences.
Series connections force single current path; parallel connections split current across multiple paths.
Electric field expressions use integration and superposition for charge distributions.
Flux measures the amount of a quantity passing through a given area.
Power depends on current and potential difference across circuit elements.
Electric flux through closed surface relates to enclosed charge.
Resistance quantifies how strongly an object opposes electric charge motion.
Circuits consist of loops containing various electrical components and measurement devices.
Dielectric materials polarize in external electric fields unlike conductors.
Electric flux through constant field equals EA cos(θ); area vector perpendicular, outward.
Electrostatic force is proportional to charges and inversely proportional to distance squared.
Net charge changes only through electron transfer between system and surroundings.
Electric field equals force per test charge; points away from positive, toward negative charges.
Symmetry simplifies electric field analysis for certain charge distributions.
Gaussian surface is a three-dimensional closed surface.
Mathematical equation describes potential energy between two charges.
Charged object motion between potentials converts energy conserving total energy.
Excess charges move to conductor surfaces in electrostatic equilibrium.
Ground serves as zero-potential reference, infinitely sourcing or absorbing charge.
Capacitance depends on charge, voltage, plate area, separation, and dielectric material.
Dielectric constant relates material permittivity to free space permittivity.
Current density describes charge flow per unit area as vector quantity related to field.
Closed circuits allow charge flow; open circuits prevent it; short circuits allow flow without potential difference.
Resistance depends on resistivity, length, area; resistivity varies with temperature and material structure.
Lightbulb brightness increases with power for qualitative prediction.
Series resistances sum directly; parallel resistances sum as reciprocals, reducing total resistance.
Loop rule follows energy conservation; potential differences sum to zero around closed loops.
Series capacitors have equal charge on all plates by conservation principle.
Magnetic fields are vectors representable using field maps.
Magnetic force on moving charges depends on velocity-field cross product.
Magnetic field circles current-carrying wire; no radial or parallel components.
Total flux through any surface is surface integral of magnetic field.
Lenz's law determines induced emf direction; opposes flux change.
Magnetic forces act only on loop segments in fields, causing acceleration.
Inductors store magnetic field energy; energy dissipation and transfer obey conservation laws.
Kirchhoff's loop rule generates differential equations describing current in LR circuits.
Capacitor charge exhibits simple harmonic motion in LC circuits.
Ohm's law relates current, voltage, resistance; ohmic materials have constant resistance and dissipate heat.
Grounding a conductor in external field induces charge redistribution on it.
Total system energy is sum of all pairwise charge interactions.
Force direction depends on charge signs: same sign repels, opposite attracts.
Magnetic field lines form closed loops per Gauss's law for magnetism.
Total flux through surface equals surface integral of electric field dot area.
Biot-Savart law derives magnetic fields around wire segments and loops.
Moving charges experience independent electric and magnetic forces simultaneously.
Induced emf relates to inductance and current rate of change via Faraday's law.
Grounding connects charged objects to large neutral systems like Earth.
Total current determined by integrating current density over conductor cross-section.
Time constant τ = L/R measures LR circuit approach to steady state exponentially.
Maxwell's equations comprehensively describe electromagnetism including induction.
Ideal batteries and wires have negligible resistance; emf is terminal voltage with no current.
Total flux independent of surface size for constant enclosed charge.
Polarized dielectric field opposes the external electric field direction.
Electric field between plates is uniform, proportional to charge density, causes constant acceleration.
Angular frequency derivable from LC circuit differential equation.
Loop force depends on induced current, flux change rate, resistance, and velocity.
Circuit elements can simultaneously belong to more than one electrical loop.
Electric field is vector quantity; net field is superposition; diagrams show magnitude and direction.
Charges reach equilibrium instantly, making conductor surfaces equipotential with non-uniform density.
RC circuit charge and current follow differential equations from Kirchhoff's loop rule.
Model nonideal battery internal resistance as series resistor with ideal battery source.
Construct surfaces where field is perpendicular or parallel to simplify integration.
Schematics represent circuits using standard symbols; physical arrangement determines circuit properties.
Maxwell's equations show electromagnetic waves travel at constant speed.
Inductor properties transition exponentially to steady state; eventually acts as conducting wire.
Current is scalar with conventional direction of positive charge movement despite electron flow.
Conductor charges reside on surface; interior field is zero; field perpendicular at surface.
Newton's second law applies to loops with induced emf in magnetic fields.
Time constant τ = RC measures charge and discharge speed in RC circuits.
Dipoles from charge motion; poles always pair and interact by distance.
Hall effect produces voltage in conductors with perpendicular magnetic fields.
Electric forces underlie macroscopic properties but contact forces are more practical.
Magnetic fields exert forces on current-carrying conductors.
Electric field inside conductors is zero; all charge resides on surface.
Stored energy equals work done by external force to separate charges.
Dielectric insertion reduces electric field in isolated capacitor.
Electrostatic forces are bidirectional; gravitational forces are always attractive.
Insulator charges distribute throughout interior during electrostatic equilibrium.
Dielectric insertion alters capacitor capacitance value.
Electric field at conductor surfaces is perpendicular to the surface.
Magnetic dipoles align with external magnetic fields.
Current through internal resistance causes terminal voltage to drop below emf.
Integrate charge density over appropriate dimensions to find total charge.
Electric potential energy in capacitor follows a specific mathematical relationship.
Capacitor voltage and current change over time, reaching steady state eventually.
Electrostatic forces are typically much stronger than gravitational forces.
External fields cause conductor polarization while maintaining equipotential conditions.
Ammeters measure current in series with zero resistance to avoid affecting circuit.
Gauss's law is the first of Maxwell's four fundamental electromagnetism equations.
Ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, and diamagnetic materials respond differently to fields.
Closed conducting shells block external electric fields from interior regions.
Gravity dominates at large scales because macroscopic systems are electrically neutral.
Earth's magnetic field approximates a magnetic dipole.
Voltmeters measure potential difference in parallel across circuit elements.
Permittivity measures how much a material polarizes in electric fields.
Magnetic permeability measures material magnetization response to fields.
Statement incomplete in provided text.
Free space has a constant magnetic permeability value.
